---
title: "Melanoma"
format: 
  revealjs:
    slide-number: c/t
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    logo: "atriumlogo.jpg"
    footer: "[GI Surgical Oncology Talks](https://gisurgonc.github.io/talks/)"
    #css: ["theme/theme.css"]
    theme: default
      
    echo: false
editor: visual
bibliography: zotero.bib
---

## T1a Melanoma = Stage 1A

SLNB is not generally recommended for melanomas \<0.8 mm thick without ulceration or other adverse features, as the probability of a positive sentinel node is \<5%.

Exception: is when there is significant uncertainty about the depth of the lesion (e.g., positive deep margins or limited sampling of a larger lesion).

## T1b Melanoma = Stage 1B

SLNB should be discussed and considered for melanomas \<0.8 mm thick with ulceration or 0.8-1.0 mm thick (with or without ulceration), as the probability of a positive sentinel node is 5-10%.

For T1a lesions \>0.5 mm with other adverse features (age ≤42 years, head/neck location, lymphovascular invasion, and/or mitotic index ≥2/mm²), SLNB should also be discussed, with additive risk when multiple adverse features are present.

## Stage IB (T2a) or Stage II (Tb2^+^)

SLNB should be discussed and offered for melanomas \>1.0 mm thick, as the probability of a positive sentinel node is generally \>10%. This includes intermediate-thickness melanomas (T2 or T3; 1.0-4.0 mm) where SLNB is recommended, and thick melanomas (T4; \>4.0 mm) where SLNB may be recommended after discussion of benefits and risks.

## Excision Margins for Primary Melanoma

| **Breslow Thickness** | **Recommended Peripheral Surgical Margin** |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| In situ               | 0.5–1 cm                                   |
| ≤1.0 mm (T1)          | 1 cm                                       |
| \>1.0–2.0 mm (T2)     | 1–2 cm                                     |
| \>2.0–4.0 mm (T3)     | 2 cm                                       |
| \>4.0 mm (T4)         | 2 cm                                       |

## Staging

| **Breslow Thickness** | **Recommended Peripheral Surgical Margin** |
|-----------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| In situ               | 0.5–1 cm                                   |
| ≤1.0 mm (T1)          | 1 cm                                       |
| \>1.0–2.0 mm (T2)     | 1–2 cm                                     |
| \>2.0–4.0 mm (T3)     | 2 cm                                       |
| \>4.0 mm (T4)         | 2 cm                                       |

## 

Node positive patients are staged with "PET/CT Whole Body." This is different from PET used for staging GI cancers, which is "PET/CT Skull Base to Mid Thigh"

High risk patients will need either MRI or CT of brain

## Treatment of Positive Sentinel Node

Active nodal basin ultrasound or other radiographic surveillance without completion lymph node dissection (CLND) is now the preferred approach for patients with positive sentinel lymph node biopsy

surveillance should include nodal ultrasound every 4 months during the first 2 years, then every 6 months during years 3 through 5,

. Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy and Management of Regional Lymph Nodes in Melanoma: American Society of Clinical Oncology and Society of Surgical Oncology Clinical Practice Guideline Update. Journal of Clinical Oncology : Official Journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology. 2018. Wong SL, Faries MB, Kennedy EB, et al.

## Sentinel Node Biopsy in Special Cases

Pure desmoplastic melanoma (≥90% of invasive melanoma with prominent stromal fibrosis) has lower SLNB positivity rates, and the role of SLNB remains controversial in this subgroup. \[1\]

SLNB may be considered for isolated in-transit metastasis or local recurrence without clinically or radiographically evident regional nodal or distant metastases, if it will affect adjuvant therapy decisions. \[1\]

## MSLT-I Clinical trial

[@morton599]

## MSLT-II Trial

Patients with positive sentinel nodes were randomized to completion lymph node dissection vs observation (with periodic ultrasound of the draining node basin)

No difference in overall survival

::: aside
[@faires2211]
:::

## DeCOG-SLT Trial

::: aside
[@faires2211]
:::

## Stage III Treatment

BRAF V600 tumor mutation testing

IIIA - T1a/b-T2a N1a OR N2a: Obervation

IIIB/C - Higher risk: adjuvant therapy

## Adjuvant Therapy - Stage IIIA

For patients with very low tumor volume stage IIIA disease (T1a/b–T2a/N1a or N2a), observation is preferred for most patients, as the toxicity of adjuvant therapy may outweigh the benefit. \[1\] Patients with stage IIIA melanoma and SLN tumor deposits ≥0.3 mm in maximum dimension are at higher risk and may benefit from adjuvant systemic therapy, while those with deposits \<0.3 mm demonstrate 5-year melanoma-specific survival similar to stage IB disease.

## Adjuvant Systemic Therapy - Stage III B/C

The decision for adjuvant therapy should be individualized based on the patient's risk of recurrence and treatment toxicity.

Nivolumab (Category 1) - studied in AJCC 7th Edition stage IIIB/C disease

Pembrolizumab (Category 1) - tested in stage IIIA with SLN metastasis ≥1 mm or stage IIIB/C disease

Dabrafenib/trametinib (Category 1) - for BRAF V600 mutation-positive melanoma

All three regimens have demonstrated clinical efficacy across AJCC 8th Edition stage III disease and are given for 12 months. \[1\] Anti-PD-1 therapy (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) has shown improved 5-year recurrence-free survival compared to placebo or ipilimumab. \[4-5\] For BRAF V600-mutant melanoma, dabrafenib/trametinib is an equally effective alternative to immunotherapy. \[4\]\[6\]

## Systemic Adjuvant Therapy

Systemic Therapy for Melanoma: ASCO Guideline Update. Journal of Clinical Oncology : Official Journal of the American Society of Clinical Oncology. 2023. Seth R, Agarwala SS, Messersmith H, et al.Guideline

## Resources

Cutaneous Melanoma. Lancet. 2023. Long GV, Swetter SM, Menzies AM, Gershenwald JE, Scolyer RA.

Cutaneous Melanoma. The Journal of the American Medical Association. 2025. Joshi UM, Kashani-Sabet M, Kirkwood JM.

# Orientation Manual

{{< qrcode https://gisurgonc.github.io/handbook width=500 height=500 >}}

# References
